speaker: Unknown
en: This design process that designers have been taught, we sort of treat it as gospel.
zh: 设计师们被教导的这种设计流程，我们有点把它当作福音。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's basically dead.
zh: 那基本上已经死了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You as a designer actually do not have the time to make these beautiful mocks anymore.
zh: 作为一名设计师，实际上你不再有时间去制作这些精美的模型了。

speaker: Unknown
en: A big part of the design role now is helping engineers and teams execute, not just telling them, "Here's the design."
zh: 现在设计角色的很大一部分是帮助工程师和团队执行，而不仅仅是告诉他们，“这是设计。”

speaker: Unknown
en: A few years ago, 60 to 70% of it was mocking and prototyping, but now I feel the mocking up part of it is 30 to 40%.
zh: 几年前，其中 60% 到 70% 是制作模型和原型，但现在我觉得制作模型这部分是 30% 到 40%。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're better off not blocking that, letting them cook.
zh: 你最好不要阻碍这一点，让他们去发挥。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not just designers who are feeling like, "Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers."
zh: 不仅仅是设计师觉得，“噢是的，我们要跟上工程师。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think even engineers are like, "How do we keep up with ourselves?"
zh: 我认为甚至工程师也在想，“我们要怎么跟上我们自己？”

speaker: Unknown
en: How to keep up with all our agents, our seven agents who are constantly running?
zh: 如何跟上我们所有的智能体，我们要不断运行的七个智能体？

speaker: Unknown
en: The result of engineering changing a bunch is that design is sort of forced to change.
zh: 工程发生很大变化的结果是，设计被迫发生了变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: We used to go off and make this two-year, five-year, 10-year vision even.
zh: 我们过去经常去制定这个两年、五年甚至十年的愿景。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now it becomes a vision that's three to six months out, and isn't necessarily creating this beautiful deck, sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
zh: 现在它变成了一个三到六个月后的愿景，并且不一定要制作这个精美的幻灯片，有时只是制作一个原型来指明方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: Boris on the podcast recently was saying Claude Code is now helping him come up with ideas.
zh: Boris 最近在播客上说，Claude Code 现在正在帮助他想出主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we'll get better at taste and judgment and design.
zh: 我们会变得更擅长品味、判断和设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: We might be holding onto that a little bit too much.
zh: 我们可能有点太死守这一点了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Where will human brains continue to be valuable?
zh: 人脑在哪里继续有价值？

speaker: Unknown
en: At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters.
zh: 归根结底，必须有人决定实际要构建什么以及什么真正重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
zh: 仍然需要对决定负责的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you now look for when you're hiring designers?
zh: 你在招聘设计师时现在看重什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's probably three archetypes of folks that are really interesting to me right now.
zh: 现在有三类人对我来说真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today's guest is Jenny Wen.
zh: 今天的嘉宾是 Jenny Wen。

speaker: Unknown
en: Jenny was head of design for Claude, is now leading design for Claude Cowork.
zh: Jenny 曾是 Claude 的设计负责人，现在负责 Claude Cowork 的设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: Prior to that, she was director of design at Figma, where she led the design teams behind FigJam and Slides.
zh: 在此之前，她是 Figma 的设计总监，在那里她领导了 FigJam 和 Slides 背后的设计团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: She was also a designer at Dropbox and Square and Shopify.
zh: 她也曾是 Dropbox、Square 和 Shopify 的设计师。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I love about this conversation is that Jenny is living in the future of where design as a profession is heading, and she's here to give us a glimpse into what that looks like, and how much things are going to be changing for designers.
zh: 我喜欢这次谈话的地方在于，Jenny 生活在这个设计行业正走向的未来，她在这里给了我们一窥那是什么样子的机会，以及设计师的事情将会发生多大的变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is pretty wild and extremely interesting.
zh: 这相当疯狂且极其有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: A huge thank you to Noah Levin and Emily Lynn Hasham for suggesting topics and questions for this conversation.
zh: 非常感谢 Noah Levin 和 Emily Lynn Hasham 为这次对话建议的主题和问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't forget to check out Lennysproductpass.com for an incredible set of deals available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 别忘了查看 Lennysproductpass.com，那里有一组专门提供给 Lenny's newsletter 订阅者的精彩优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's get into it after a short word from our wonderful sponsors.
zh: 在我们精彩的赞助商简短致辞后，让我们开始吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Mercury, radically different banking loved by over 300,000 entrepreneurs, including me.
zh: 这一集由 Mercury 赞助，这是一种深受超过 30 万名企业家（包括我）喜爱的截然不同的银行业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: I switched to Mercury from Chase over a year ago, and it is such a profoundly better experience.
zh: 一年多前我从 Chase 转到了 Mercury，这是一种好得多的体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like an actual product person built a bank, versus a banking person building a product.
zh: 这就像是一个真正的产品人建立了一家银行，而不是银行人建立一个产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is fast, it's elegant, it is super easy to send wires, to track my spending, to set up triggers, to move money around when accounts get low.
zh: 它很快，它很优雅，它超级容易发送电汇，追踪我的支出，设置触发器，在账户余额不足时转移资金。

speaker: Unknown
en: We moved all of our invoicing to Mercury, and it is such a smoother experience than anything else we've tried.
zh: 我们将所有的发票业务都转移到了 Mercury，这比我们尝试过的任何其他东西都要顺畅。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's also really easy to grant people on your team just the right amount of access to help take work off your plate.
zh: 给你团队里的人授予恰到好好的访问权限来帮你分担工作也非常容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's free to get started.
zh: 开始使用是免费的。

speaker: Unknown
en: No in-person visits, no minimum balances.
zh: 不需要亲自拜访，没有最低余额要求。

speaker: Unknown
en: The product also flexes to all sizes of company, from startups to large enterprises.
zh: 该产品还适应所有规模的公司，从初创公司到大型企业。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just visit mercury.com to learn more and apply online in minutes.
zh: 只需访问 mercury.com 了解更多并在几分钟内在线申请。

speaker: Unknown
en: Mercury is a FinTech company, not an FDIC insured bank, banking services provided through Choice Financial Group and Column NA, members FDIC.
zh: Mercury 是一家 FinTech 公司，而不是 FDIC 承保的银行，银行服务通过 Choice Financial Group 和 Column NA 提供，他们是 FDIC 的成员。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Orkes, the company behind Open Source Conductor, the orchestration platform powering modern enterprise applications.
zh: 这一集由 Orkes 赞助，它是开源 Conductor 背后的公司，Conductor 是为现代企业应用程序提供动力的编排平台。

speaker: Unknown
en: Modern systems are built on microservices, APIs, and event-driven architectures, but legacy automation tools can't keep up.
zh: 现代系统建立在微服务、API 和事件驱动架构之上，但遗留的自动化工具无法跟上。

speaker: Unknown
en: Siloed low-code platforms, outdated process management, and disconnected API tooling breakdown under real world scale and constant change.
zh: 孤岛式的低代码平台、过时的流程管理和断开连接的 API 工具在现实世界的规模和不断的变化下崩溃。

speaker: Unknown
en: Orkes Conductor provides a production grade orchestration layer for coordinating microservices, APIs, data pipelines, human tasks, and agentic workflows, with deterministic control flow, retries, observability and governance.
zh: Orkes Conductor 为协调微服务、API、数据管道、人工任务和智能体工作流提供了一个生产级编排层，具有确定性的控制流、重试、可观测性和治理。

speaker: Unknown
en: Built for enterprise scale, Orkes supports visual and code first development with built-in compliance and reliability.
zh: 为企业规模而构建，Orkes 支持可视化和代码优先开发，具有内置的合规性和可靠性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Through a built-in MCP gateway, AI agents handle reasoning and decision-making while safely accessing existing APIs and internal systems as MCP tools.
zh: 通过内置的 MCP 网关，AI 智能体处理推理和决策，同时安全地访问现有的 API 和内部系统作为 MCP 工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: This enables agents to operate across enterprise environments, and scale from demos to production, orchestrating systems, agents, and humans together, to deliver smarter outcomes faster.
zh: 这使智能体能够跨越企业环境运作，并从演示扩展到生产，编排系统、智能体和人类，以更快地交付更智能的结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: Learn more at Orkes.io/lenny.
zh: 在 Orkes.io/lenny 了解更多。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's O-R-K-E-S.io/lenny.
zh: 就是 O-R-K-E-S.io/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: Jenny, thank you so much for being here, and welcome to the podcast.
zh: Jenny，非常感谢你来到这里，欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, excited to be here.
zh: 是的，很高兴来到这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've been looking forward to this conversation because I spend a lot of time on this podcast talking about the future of software engineering, how much that role is changing, the role of product management, how much that role is changing.
zh: 我一直期待这次对话，因为我在这个播客上花了很多时间谈论软件工程的未来，这个角色在多大程度上正在改变，产品经理的角色，以及这个角色在多大程度上正在改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: I even spent a lot of time on how design is changing.
zh: 我甚至花了很多时间谈论设计是如何变化的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Clearly, it is also changing in a really big way.
zh: 显然，它也正在以一种非常大的方式发生变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have such a front row seat to where things are heading.
zh: 而且你对事态发展的走向拥有最前线的观察视角。

speaker: Unknown
en: I also know you have a lot of very strong opinions about where things are heading, so there's a lot of stuff I want to talk about.
zh: 我也知道你对事态发展的走向有很多非常强烈的看法，所以有很多我想谈论的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to just start with just this broad question.
zh: 我想先从这个宽泛的问题开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: How is the design process changing with the rise of AI?
zh: 随着 AI 的兴起，设计流程是如何变化的？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's changing a lot.
zh: 它变化很大。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's still also got a long way to go in terms of the way it's changing.
zh: 我认为就其变化的方式而言，它还有很长的路要走。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think we've actually seen engineering change a lot more in the past little while than design actually has, but I think the result of engineering changing a bunch is that design is sort of forced to change.
zh: 我认为实际上在过去的一段时间里，我们看到工程的变化比设计多得多，但我认为工程发生大量变化的结果是，设计被迫发生变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think some context around this, is I did a talk at a conference in Berlin a few months ago in September, and I called it Don't Trust the Design Process, where I basically just said, "Hey, you know this design process that designers have been taught, where you go and you go off and you do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, you converge, diverge, converge."
zh: 所以我想对此提供一些背景，几个月前，也就是九月，我在柏林的一个会议上做了一次演讲，我称之为《不要相信设计流程》，我基本上只是说，“嘿，你知道设计师们所受教的这个设计流程，你去那里，做一堆研究和发现，然后你发散，你收敛，发散，收敛。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like this process that we sort of treated as gospel and tried so hard to preserve and we were like, "Trust the process."
zh: 这就像是我们将其视为福音并努力保护的流程，我们就像是，“相信流程。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's basically dead.
zh: 那基本上已经死了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it was sort of dying before the age of AI, but given now that engineers can go off and spin off their seven Claudes, I think as designers, we really have to let go of that process.
zh: 我认为在 AI 时代之前它就已经有点消亡了，但既然现在工程师可以离开并启动他们的七个 Claudes，我认为作为设计师，我们真的必须放弃那个流程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's the big thing that's changing.
zh: 我认为这就是正在发生重大变化的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think even in the past three to four months since I did that talk, that talk actually starts to feel pretty ... It kind of feels outdated to me, which is a little embarrassing, but especially with the big shift of Opus 46 and a bunch of folks just really discovering and using Claude Code over the holiday break, I think we're seeing this force to change our process happen even more.
zh: 但我认为，自从我做那次演讲以来的过去三到四个月里，那次演讲实际上开始感觉非常……它对我来说感觉有点过时了，这有点尴尬，但特别是随着 Opus 46 的大转变，以及很多人在假期期间真正发现并使用 Claude Code，我认为我们看到改变我们流程的压力甚至更大了。

speaker: Unknown
en: The way I see it now is there are basically two types of design work, and design work is becoming really stratified in this new world.
zh: 我现在看待它的方式是基本上有两种类型的设计工作，在这个新世界中，设计工作变得非常分层。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's the first one, which is really just supporting the implementation and execution.
zh: 所以有第一种，它实际上只是支持和执行。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this is the one where engineers are using their seven Claudes to create all these features, and anybody can put an idea out there, and you can just talk about an idea and somebody, usually actually an engineer because they're still better at implementing this stuff than we are, they will just make a scrappy version of it and you can try it out.
zh: 这就是工程师们使用他们的七个 Claudes 来创建所有这些功能的情况，任何人都可以在那里提出一个想法，你可以只是谈论一个想法，然后某人，通常实际上是工程师，因为他们仍然比我们更擅长实现这些东西，他们只是制作一个粗糙的版本，然后你可以尝试一下。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you as a designer actually do not have the time to make these beautiful mocks anymore, or to lead in this way.
zh: 作为设计师，你实际上没有时间再制作这些漂亮的模型了，也没有时间以这种方式主导了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I think there's the second kind of work that feels also really important, which is creating the sort of vision or direction for things.
zh: 我认为还有第二种工作也感觉非常重要，那就是为事物创造某种愿景或方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: This one feels like the hardest to make time for, and it's one that we still did before, but I think the shape of it's very much changing, because I think we used to go off and say, "We're going to do this design vision.
zh: 这一种感觉是最难腾出时间来做的，这也是我们以前做过的事情，但我认为它的形状正在发生很大的变化，因为我认为我们过去常说，“我们要做这个设计愿景。”

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to go off and make this two-year, five-year, whatever, 10-year vision even, and we're going to point us towards something."
zh: “我们将去制定这个两年、五年，不管是什么，甚至十年的愿景，我们将指向某件事。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But the way that the technology is changing now, we don't know what's going to happen in two years.
zh: 但是鉴于技术现在的变化方式，我们不知道两年后会发生什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's too much changing, and it usually becomes a vision that's three to six months out, and isn't necessarily something that is creating this beautiful deck that's beautifully story told.
zh: 变化太多了，而且它通常变成一个三到六个月后的愿景，不一定是创造一个美丽的、故事讲得很漂亮的演示文稿。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
zh: 有时它只是创建一个原型，为人们指出正确的方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think this kind of work is still really important in this world because in a world where people can spin off their seven Claudes, make whatever features they want in any kind of direction or in implementation, you need to point them towards something.
zh: 我认为这种工作在这个世界中仍然非常重要，因为在一个人们可以启动他们的七个 Claudes、在任何方向或实现中制作他们想要的任何功能的世界里，你需要指引他们朝向某件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in order to make sure that we're all making something that makes sense together and is also done in a way where it's efficient.
zh: 而且为了确保我们都在制造某种共同有意义的东西，并且也是以一种高效的方式完成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: If we're all working towards something that has one greater cause, it's much more efficient to do that than just random things.
zh: 如果我们都在为一个更伟大的目标而努力，这样做比仅仅做随机的事情要高效得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that's the big shift that I'm seeing.
zh: 这就是我正在看到的重大转变。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think I have opinions about it now, but ask me in three months and it might actually change even more.
zh: 我想我现在对此有看法，但三个月后再问我，它实际上可能会发生更多变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what you're saying here is it's not like you or the design field is like, "We need to change?"
zh: 所以你在这里说的并不是说，你或设计领域就像是说，“我们需要改变？”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's engineering and the fact that you can build so quickly, just forces the role of a designer to change because as you said, engineers can just ship, ship, ship, ship, ship.
zh: 是工程以及你可以如此快速地构建这一事实，只是迫使设计师的角色发生改变，因为正如你所说，工程师可以只是交付，交付，交付，交付，交付。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what you're finding here is you're better off not blocking that, letting them cook, as they say.
zh: 你在这里发现的是，你最好不要阻止它，让他们去发挥，正如他们所说的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's this mode of helping them along as they ship, bring it together, make sure it all connects, guide them a little bit.
zh: 然后有一种模式是在他们交付的过程中帮助他们，把它们整合在一起，确保它们都连接起来，稍微引导他们一下。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think so.
zh: 是的，我是这么认为的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think there is one unifying voice that's like, "Designers, we need to change right now."
zh: 我不认为有一个统一的声音像是说，“设计师们，我们现在需要改变。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But yeah, there is the follow-on effects of engineering tooling really changing.
zh: 但是是的，有工程工具真正变化的连锁反应。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think we'll probably see design tooling change in this next year or so as well, but a lot of it right now is trailing that.
zh: 我们可能也会在明年左右看到设计工具发生变化，但目前很多工作都落后于它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's also really empowering for us too, because as designers, we also now have access to a lot of these coding tools.
zh: 我认为这对我们也非常有益，因为作为设计师，我们现在也可以访问很多这些编码工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we can be a part of the process in a way where we're implementing stuff.
zh: 我们可以以一种我们正在实现东西的方式参与到这个过程中。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm doing a lot of last mile stuff where I'm implementing all the polish, and working with engineers really closely to get the feature across the line, and also prototype stuff in actual code as opposed to relying on engineers to do that again.
zh: 我正在做很多最后一英里的工作，我在实现所有的润色，并与工程师密切合作以完成功能，并且还用实际的代码制作原型，而不是再次依赖工程师来做这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: How true do you think this is at all companies, at say AI companies, non-AI companies?
zh: 你认为这在所有公司，比如说 AI 公司、非 AI 公司中有多真实？

speaker: Unknown
en: Someone may be hearing this, okay, Anthropic Claude, okay, they're at the bleeding edge, for one.
zh: 有人可能会听到这个，好吧，Anthropic Claude，好吧，他们处于最前沿，首先。

speaker: Unknown
en: Two, it's developery a little bit, but I think people might be feeling like, "Okay, this is not going to happen at Salesforce.
zh: 其次，它有点偏向开发人员，但我认为人们可能会觉得，“好吧，这不会在 Salesforce 发生。”

speaker: Unknown
en: This is not going to happen at, I don't know, ServiceNow, wherever."
zh: 这不会在，我不知道，ServiceNow，无论哪里发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I guess, do you feel like this is where all teams are heading?
zh: 所以我想问，你觉得这是所有团队正在走向的方向吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it mostly AI, bleeding edge companies?
zh: 它主要是 AI 公司、最前沿的公司吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: How widespread do you think the design process shift is going to be?
zh: 你认为这种设计流程的转变会有多广泛？

speaker: Unknown
en: So the talk that I did last year has really been the most resonant talk that I've done.
zh: 所以我去年做的那个演讲，真的是我做过的最能引起共鸣的演讲。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think it's something that people are starting to feel across the industry, where they're like, "Oh yeah, we can't do the old design process anymore.
zh: 所以我认为这是全行业的人们开始感觉到的事情，他们会说，“是的，我们不能再沿用旧的设计流程了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are using tools like Claude Code and v0 and whatnot, to start to spin up prototypes, and PMs are starting to spin up prototypes and stuff like that as well."

speaker: Unknown

en: I think you sort of have to choose and use your discretion as to when to actually ship something, but I think the ability to execute, try something out and try it with real data, and a real user's kind of mindset in the product, I think that does lead to a better product, especially as we're all working with these new developing AI models that are non-deterministic, you can't mock up all the states, and you can't theorize and you can't even make a clickable prototype with it.
zh: 我认为你某种程度上必须选择并运用你的判断力来决定何时真正发布东西，但我认为执行能力、尝试一些东西并用真实数据和产品中用户真实的心态去尝试，我认为这确实能带来更好的产品，特别是当我们都在使用这些非确定性的新兴 AI 模型时，你不能把所有的状态都做成模型图，你也不能理论化，你甚至不能用它制作一个可点击的原型。

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: So Figma has been really great at just exploring all these different options, and I think it's still going to exist that way to some extent.
zh: 所以 Figma 在探索所有这些不同选项方面真的非常棒，我认为它在某种程度上仍然会那样存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I think there's really fine visual and interaction details that are also really great to be able to just try out in Figma.
zh: 然后我认为真的很好的视觉和交互细节，在 Figma 中直接试用也真的很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, it's a lot of different directions, but it's micro directions.
zh: 同样，这是很多不同的方向，但是是微观的方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's being able to think about different typography or styles.
zh: 它是能够考虑不同的排版或风格。

speaker: Unknown
en: Having those in a canvas where you can just explore that specifically is still so, so helpful, and is not something that I always want to go directly to code in.
zh: 在一个画布上拥有这些东西，你可以专门地探索那个，仍然是非常、非常有帮助的，而且这不是我总是想直接去代码里做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting you still use an IDE, because in engineering, it's clearly shifting to command lines, agents, IDEs are kind of moving to not be cool anymore.
zh: 你仍然使用 IDE 这一点很有趣，因为在工程领域，显然正在转向命令行、智能体，IDE 有点变得不再酷了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it makes a lot of sense.
zh: 这很有道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just want to edit some CSS things, some color stuff.
zh: 你只是想编辑一些 CSS 的东西，一些颜色的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I could see why not just telling the agent, "Hey, just come on, change this one hex value."
zh: 所以我能理解为什么不直接告诉智能体，“嘿，来吧，改掉这个十六进制值。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Just changing it is so much easier.
zh: 只是改变它要容易得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really annoying to be like, "Can you change this to this class?"
zh: 不得不说“能不能把这个改成这个类？”真的很烦。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you can just go in and change it to a different class.
zh: 当你可以直接进去把它改成另一个类的时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's interesting.
zh: 所以这很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wonder if IDEs now become the useful for designers and PMs, and engineers have moved on?
zh: 我想知道 IDE 现在是否变得对设计师和 PM 有用了，而工程师已经转移了？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, maybe.
zh: 是的，也许吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So a lot of your time you spend working with engineers, giving feedback, nudging them in the right direction.
zh: 所以你很多时间都花在与工程师一起工作，提供反馈，推动他们朝正确的方向前进。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a sense, I feel, of just your advice is let go.
zh: 有一种感觉，我觉得，只是你的建议是放手。

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't feel like you need to be this gatekeeper, but there's this piece of, okay, help them move in a direction that is cohesive and is creating products we're proud of.
zh: 不要觉得你需要成为这个守门人，但有这么一点，好的，帮助他们朝着一个连贯的方向前进，创造我们引以为豪的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of designers I think are in this boat right now, just like, "Oh my God, I can't keep up with all these engineers shipping stuff all day."
zh: 我想很多设计师现在都在这条船上，就像，“天哪，我无法跟上这些工程师整天发布东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: What's something you learned about just either how to help your engineers get better at design so that it just ends up being better, or just keeping on top of this and not going crazy?
zh: 你学到了什么关于如何帮助你的工程师在设计上变得更好，以便结果变得更好，或者仅仅是保持掌控而不发疯？

speaker: Unknown
en: Whenever I do work with engineers on projects, and it's more on a consulting basis, I do just try to explain why I'm thinking a way that I'm thinking, to help them extract principles.
zh: 每当我在项目上与工程师合作时，而且更多是在咨询的基础上，我确实只是试图解释我为什么这样思考，以帮助他们提取原则。

speaker: Unknown
en: As opposed to me just being like, "No, I don't think this should go here."
zh: 而不是我只是说，“不，我不认为这应该放在这里。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "No, I think we should have a button here because not everybody realizes you can prompt this."
zh: 而是，“不，我认为我们应该在这里有一个按钮，因为不是每个人都意识到你可以提示这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And here's an example where it comes from research and whatnot.
zh: 这里有一个来自研究等方面的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I also just try to point engineers to our design system and stuff like that in code, because right now Claude is writing a lot of the code and it's not always picking up stuff in the design system and whatnot.
zh: 所以我也只是试着将工程师指向我们的设计系统和代码中的类似东西，因为现在 Claude 编写了大量的代码，但它并不总是获取设计系统中的东西等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: So as much as I can equip them with stuff that they can use in the future without me, if that's helpful.
zh: 所以只要我能用他们在没有我的情况下未来也能使用的东西来装备他们，如果这有用的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then on your point of trying not to go crazy, I think it's hard.
zh: 然后关于你提到的努力不要发疯这一点，我觉得很难。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's really hard right now.
zh: 我觉得现在真的很难。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I see this a lot from actually both engineers and designers, where it's like now that we're sort of capable of doing so much, we want to do more.
zh: 我从实际上工程师和设计师双方都看到了很多这种情况，就是既然我们现在有能力做这么多事，我们想做更多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think it's not just designers who are feeling like, oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.
zh: 所以我认为不仅仅是设计师觉得“哦，是的，我们必须跟上工程师”。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think even engineers are like, "How do we keep up with ourselves right now?"
zh: 我认为即使是工程师也在想，“我们现在如何跟上我们自己的步伐？”

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's something I'm hearing a lot.
zh: 所以这是我经常听到的一件事。

speaker: Lenny
en: So true. Oh man, how to keep up with all our agents, our seven agents we're constantly running?
zh: 太真实了。天哪，如何跟上我们要不断运行的所有智能体，我们要运行的七个智能体？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then as a designer where in this profession, craft and great experience and quality and trust are such a core part of the job, to help instill that in the products, because that in theory leads to really successful products in companies, how do you just think about maintaining craft quality, trust, as your products are just shipping 1,000 times a day, and you're not able to stay on top of them and there's no designer involved?
zh: 那么作为一名设计师，在这个职业中，工艺、卓越的体验、质量和信任是工作的核心部分，为了帮助将其灌输到产品中，因为理论上这会导致真正成功的产品和公司，你如何考虑保持工艺质量、信任度，当你的产品每天发布 1000 次，而你无法掌控它们，并且没有设计师参与时？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not that there's no designer involved.
zh: 并不是说没有设计师参与。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's more just like it's almost that there's too much for one designer to handle.
zh: 更多只是说几乎有太多东西让一个设计师无法处理。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think with this, I think about where the features or products are, where they are in the cycle of adoption versus early preview.
zh: 但我认为对于这一点，我考虑的是功能或产品在哪里，它们在采用周期与早期预览中的位置。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, we sometimes will launch things and we will say, "Hey, this is a research preview.
zh: 例如，我们有时会发布东西，我们会说，“嘿，这是一个研究预览版。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's early.
zh: 它还很早期。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's going to have a bunch of these flaws," and we caveat that a bunch.
zh: 它会有很多这些缺陷，”我们要对此做很多说明。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think Claude Cowork is actually a good example of this, where we labeled it a research preview and we put it out there knowing that, "Hey, this is similar to our models.
zh: 我认为 Claude Cowork 实际上就是一个很好的例子，我们将其标记为研究预览版并将其推出，我们知道，“嘿，这类似于我们的模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is the worst it's ever going to be, but we're going to put it out there because we believe, internally we've tried it a bunch, and there's something really powerful here that some people will benefit from.
zh: 这是它将会呈现的最糟糕状态，但我们要把它发布出去，因为我们相信，我们在内部已经尝试了很多次，这里有一些真正强大的东西，有些人会从中受益。

speaker: Unknown
en: It might not yet be the easiest to work with.
zh: 它可能还不是最容易使用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It might not be the highest quality.
zh: 它可能不是最高质量的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It might have some issues with it, but we're going to put it out there because we believe the benefits outweigh the cons."
zh: 它可能有一些问题，但我们要把它发布出去，因为我们认为利大于弊。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that is okay to do, especially when there is something really valuable with the product already, and it's worth putting it out there.
zh: 我认为这是可以做的，特别是当产品已经有一些真正有价值的东西时，值得把它推向世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think the promise you sort of have to make your users is like, "Hey, we're going to put it out there, but we're going to iterate.
zh: 但我认为你必须向用户做出的承诺是，“嘿，我们会把它发布出去，但我们会进行迭代。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to take your feedback and we're going to iterate and we're going to make it better."
zh: 我们会听取你们的反馈，我们会进行迭代，我们会让它变得更好。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have to commit that.
zh: 你必须做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to show that to the world, you have to respond to people's feedback, and you have to show that you are continuously shipping and improving it.
zh: 你必须向世界展示这一点，你必须回应人们的反馈，你必须表明你正在持续地发布和改进它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because I think the way that you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early, is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens.
zh: 因为我认为你真正在质量和发布早期版本方面失去信任的方式是，如果你发布得很早，然后什么都没发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is something that degrades a brand.
zh: 这是损害品牌的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to do that and maintain the brand of your company.
zh: 但是，只要你早期发布东西，就有可能做到这一点并保持公司的品牌形象。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's something that we've been doing pretty well.
zh: 我认为这是我们一直做得很好的一件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think anyone who's listening can take away from it, it's like, yeah, well, we're continuing to do that.
zh: 我认为任何正在听的人都可以从中借鉴，那就是，是的，我们会继续这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that is actually really fun for me as a designer, because you put something out there and you actually learn and you get feedback about it immediately, and you know what to do next.
zh: 我认为作为设计师这对我来说真的很有趣，因为你把东西发布出去，你实际上会立即学习和获得关于它的反馈，并且你知道下一步该做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: The way I've heard you describe this is building trust through speed.
zh: 我听到你描述这一点的方式是通过速度建立信任。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, for sure.
zh: 是的，当然。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it's building trust through speed, but also just making people feel like they've been heard and that we're fixing things based on what they're trying to use it for, and their feedback is actually appreciated and used.
zh: 是的，这是通过速度建立信任，但也只是让人们觉得他们被倾听了，我们正在根据他们试图使用它的方式来修复问题，并且他们的反馈实际上得到了重视和使用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it's clear when the labs launch stuff, and you all are very good at this, everyone on the team is tweeting and just responding to tweets and comments and then shipping, "Hey, we fixed this yesterday and this is happening."
zh: 是的，当实验室发布东西时这一点很明显，你们非常擅长这个，团队里的每个人都在发推文，只是回复推文和评论，然后发布，“嘿，我们昨天修复了这个，这正在发生。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's a clear sense of, "This is just today and we know this is broke and we will fix it."
zh: 所以有一种明显的感觉，“这只是今天，我们知道这坏了，我们会修复它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then because Claude Code can code very quickly, the fixes come very fast.
zh: 然后因为 Claude Code 可以非常快速地编写代码，修复来得非常快。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So another big question that people are asking that I ask a lot on this podcast, is around just what skills become valuable?
zh: 所以人们问的另一个大问题，也是我在这个播客上经常问的，是关于哪些技能变得有价值？

speaker: Unknown
en: And another way I've been thinking about it, Lex put it this way recently, is where will human brains continue to be valuable as AI gets smarter?
zh: 我思考这个问题的另一种方式，Lex 最近是这样说的，那就是随着 AI 变得更聪明，人脑在哪里将继续有价值？

speaker: Unknown
en: So we've gone through this progression of tab completing [inaudible 00:27:56] segments of code, to 100% of code is written by AI now, it's crazy, to now AI is reviewing its own code.
zh: 所以我们经历了这个进展，从代码片段的 Tab 补全 [听不清 00:27:56]，到 100% 的代码现在由 AI 编写，这太疯狂了，再到现在 AI 正在审查它自己的代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: Boris on the podcast recently was saying Claude Code is now helping them come up with ideas and decide what to build, which is like, okay, wow, look at that.
zh: Boris 最近在播客上说，Claude Code 现在正在帮助他们想出主意并决定要构建什么，这就像，好的，哇，看看那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Look at it go.
zh: 看它运行。

speaker: Unknown
en: The whole product workflows, the product development process slowly get eaten up by AI.
zh: 整个产品工作流程，产品开发过程慢慢地被 AI 蚕食。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the question is just where will human brains still be useful, at least until we have super intelligence?
zh: 所以问题只是人脑在哪里仍然有用，至少在我们拥有超级智能之前？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you think AI is going to get very, very good at taste, judgment, design?
zh: 你认为 AI 会在品味、判断、设计方面变得非常非常好吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it will get better at taste and judgment and design.
zh: 我认为它会在品味、判断和设计方面变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think we might be holding onto that a little bit too much and saying, "Oh yeah, a designer or somebody will always know the best thing to ship or the best version of this."
zh: 是的，我认为我们可能过于坚持这一点了，并说，“哦，是的，设计师或某人总是会知道发布的最好的东西或这个的最佳版本。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But I do think AI's sense of taste will get better.
zh: 但我确实认为 AI 的品味会变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters.
zh: 归根结底，总得有人来决定到底要构建什么，以及什么才是真正重要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when I think about people saying, "Oh, AI is just going to build this software for us," a lot of the hard parts of building software are actually not building it.
zh: 当我想起人们说，“噢，AI 会替我们构建这个软件”时，构建软件中许多困难的部分实际上并不在于构建它。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about the hardest times that you've had at work, Lenny, it's probably things like, oh, you and some other person disagreeing about what should go into this feature or what shouldn't go into this feature.
zh: 如果你想想工作中最艰难的时刻，Lenny，可能比如是你和另一个人在这个功能应该包含什么或不应该包含什么上意见不一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: And those things still feel like, yes, AI can weigh in, but it can't necessarily solve this dispute between you and somebody else.
zh: 而这些事情给人的感觉仍然是，是的，AI 可以发表意见，但它不一定能解决你和另一个人之间的这场争端。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there is something about deciding what actually goes into the things we build, which I guess is taste in some way, but maybe not taste in the way we think about aesthetic taste or whatnot.
zh: 所以，关于决定我们构建的东西里到底要包含什么，这有点事情，我猜这在某种程度上就是品味，但也可能不是我们所想的那种审美品味之类的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's some sort of, it's judgment around what to do next.
zh: 这是某种对于接下来做什么的判断。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just watching how quickly AI took over coding, which, I think a year ago, definitely two years ago, most people are like, "I don't think so.
zh: 只是看着 AI 接管编码的速度有多快，我觉得一年前，肯定是两年前，大多数人都说，“我不这么认为。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think AI will get this good."
zh: 我不认为 AI 会变得这么好。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that the best engineers in the world trusted so much, they're not even looking at the code anymore.
zh: 而且世界上最优秀的工程师非常信任它，他们甚至都不再看代码了。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's where we've gone.
zh: 这就是我们现在所处的阶段。

speaker: Unknown
en: It just made me reevaluate all these assumptions I've had about, okay, AI will never be as good as really good PMs, designers, at judging what is great and deciding what to build.
zh: 这让我重新评估了我所有的这些假设，好吧，AI 永远不会像真正优秀的 PM（产品经理）、设计师那样，在判断什么是伟大的以及决定构建什么方面那么出色。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I'm just starting to think, I think it will get there.
zh: 但我只是开始认为，我认为它会做到那一点的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Even an example you've shared, it could give these two people trying to make a decision, "Here's all of the data you need to make a decision and here's why this is the right answer and just press yes, press one, and I'll go ahead and build this."
zh: 即使是你分享的一个例子，它也可以给这两个试图做决定的人，“这是做决定所需的所有数据，这也是为什么这是正确答案的原因，只需按 yes，按 1，我就继续去构建这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think to your point, I think we undervalue just how good it'll get at this stuff.
zh: 所以我认为关于你的观点，我认为我们低估了它在这方面会变得多么出色。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So your sense is it'll get better, but your sense is we'll still need awesome designers to be involved, us and PMs, to help make these decisions, engineers, of course?
zh: 所以你的感觉是它会变得更好，但你的感觉是我们仍然需要优秀的设计师参与进来，我们和 PM，来帮助做出这些决定，当然还有工程师？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think someone will still have to decide, oh, we want to build this kind of product.
zh: 我认为总得有人来决定，噢，我们要构建这种产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or given what the AI is presenting us, someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
zh: 或者鉴于 AI 展示给我们的东西，仍然需要有人对决定负责。

speaker: Unknown
en: The same way that even though Claude can write all this code for you today, it is still an engineer who's accountable for, does that code actually work?
zh: 就像即使 Claude 今天可以为你编写所有这些代码，仍然是一个工程师对代码是否真正有效负责。

speaker: Unknown
en: Does this actually make sense in the product?
zh: 这在产品中真的有意义吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think there's that decision making/judgment layer, which feels like maybe one day will come when we won't have to do that, but it still falls on us.
zh: 所以我认为有这个决策/判断层，感觉也许有一天会到来，那时我们不必再做这些，但这仍然落在我们身上。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't make sense.
zh: 这说不通。

speaker: Unknown
en: It makes me think about the radiology example, where there's always the sense that AI is going to take over that field of radiology and tell you what is going on.
zh: 这让我想到了那个放射学的例子，人们总是觉得 AI 将接管放射学这个领域并告诉你发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the human is mostly useful for signing off on the decision, because someone needs to be liable if they're wrong.
zh: 但是人类对于签署决定主要是有用的，因为如果他们错了，需要有人承担责任。

speaker: Unknown
en: Which isn't the best job in the world, but that's a different game as [inaudible 00:31:38] code.
zh: 这不是世界上最好的工作，但那是另一场游戏了，正如代码中所述 [听不清 00:31:38]。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Another ongoing question in AI and design is just, it feels like chatbots and terminals are just like, I don't think anyone expected this to be the lasting user interface to AI.
zh: AI 和设计中另一个持续存在的问题只是，感觉聊天机器人和终端就像，我认为没人预料到这会是 AI 持久的用户界面。

speaker: Unknown
en: Chatbots, okay, no, no, this is just a temporary stop along the journey, but now it's gone even further, and just terminals.
zh: 聊天机器人，好吧，不，不，这只是旅程中的一个临时站点，但现在它走得更远了，只是终端。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have thoughts on just, I don't know, do you think there will be a next step of how we interface with AI, or do you think chatbots and terminals are mostly where we end up?
zh: 你有什么想法吗，我不知道，你认为我们与 AI 交互的方式会有下一步吗，还是你认为聊天机器人和终端主要是我们的终点？

speaker: Unknown
en: There will likely be a combination of both, both UIs and interfaces that you are interacting with, clicking with, and that feel more tactile., We are already seeing this and playing with this within Claude, the chatbot.
zh: 可能会有两者的结合，既有你与之交互、点击的 UI 和界面，感觉更具触感，我们已经在 Claude，那个聊天机器人中看到并体验了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we recently released a bunch of these widgets that let Claude elicit and ask you questions, and also show you things like the weather and stocks and whatnot in interactive ways.
zh: 所以我们最近发布了一系列小部件，让 Claude 能够引出并向你提问，并以交互方式向你展示天气和股票之类的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think those have had a really good reception, because people still like to see UIs and touch them and click them, and they are much more efficient than typing something to Claude.
zh: 我认为这些反响很好，因为人们仍然喜欢看 UI 并触摸和点击它们，这比向 Claude 输入内容效率高得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: But at the same time, when we really leaned into this chatbot paradigm, I think that just gave us this whole world of flexibility that we didn't get with these sort of baked-in UIs.
zh: 但与此同时，当我们真正深入这种聊天机器人范式时，我认为这给了我们整个灵活性的世界，这是我们在这些内置的 UI 中没有得到的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So my read here is I don't think Chat is ever going away because this opened up this new way of infinite ways to work with the model, and to talk to the computer, that we just didn't have before.
zh: 所以我的看法是，我认为 Chat 永远不会消失，因为这开启了与模型工作和与计算机交谈的无限新方式，这是我们以前没有过的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think that it will still be most direct for very specific things to exist in this UI.
zh: 但我认为，对于非常具体的事情存在于这个 UI 中，仍然是最直接的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that what will probably happen here, is that a lot of those UIs will be generated more and more often by the models, as opposed to something that we're hand coding each instance.
zh: 我认为这里可能会发生的是，这些 UI 中的大多数将越来越多地由模型生成，而不是我们要为每个实例手工编码。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think we're in this space where I don't think chat ... And maybe even talking to the terminal is going to go away.
zh: 但我认为我们处于这个空间，我认为聊天不会……甚至可能包括与终端交谈都不会消失。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting that with OpenClaw, Claude, Moltbot, all the names, one of the big innovations is another way to chat with it through WhatsApp and Telegram and SMS, just like another form of chatbot, but just like, that was a big [inaudible 00:33:54].
zh: 有趣的是，对于 OpenClaw，Claude，Moltbot，所有的名字，主要的创新之一是通过 WhatsApp 和 Telegram 以及 SMS 与它聊天的另一种方式，就像另一种形式的聊天机器人，但这就像，那是一个很大的 [听不清 00:33:54]。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, I could just chat with it through WhatsApp.
zh: 噢，我可以直接通过 WhatsApp 和它聊天。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like chatting and talking to someone is still ... We as humans are doing it, and it's a way for us to interact in a really rich way.
zh: 就像聊天和与人交谈仍然是……我们人类正在这样做，这是我们以非常丰富的方式互动的一种方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now we just have this other medium to interact with a computer, basically.
zh: 现在我们只是有了另一种与计算机交互的媒介，基本上。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So Kevin Wheel, who works at another AI lab I won't mention, he had this great point on the podcast that talking is such a beautiful way to handle every level of intelligence.
zh: 所以 Kevin Wheel，他在另一家我不便提及的 AI 实验室工作，他在播客上提出了一个很好的观点，即谈话是处理每个智力水平的一种绝美方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: We can talk to people that are very, very smart and not so smart and it's talking, and it scales so well across the spectrum.
zh: 我们可以与非常聪明和不太聪明的人交谈，而且是在交谈，它在整个范围内扩展得很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: We can talk to people at 200 IQ, 300, like its talking still works.
zh: 我们可以和智商 200、300 的人交谈，就像交谈仍然有效一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's why it's been this beautiful way to deal with the growing intelligence of models as it continues to work.
zh: 所以这就是为什么它一直是应对模型智能增长的美好方式，因为它持续有效。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that totally makes sense.
zh: 是的，完全有道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

zh: 本期节目由 Omni 赞助。

zh: 困难的部分显而易见。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to come back to this whole idea of management and IC.
zh: 我想回到管理和 IC 的整个想法上来。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you've kind of put yourself back into the IC role in a lot of ways.
zh: 所以你在很多方面把自己重新放回了 IC 的角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about that.
zh: 谈谈这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you think that's just a thing design managers need to be doing.
zh: 以及如果你认为这只是设计管理者需要做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I have takes on this.
zh: 是的，我对这个有看法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this past year at Anthropic, I joined as an IC at first, and then I managed a team for a few months in an org structure that sort of needed it, and now I'm actually back to doing full-time IC work.
zh: 所以今年在 Anthropic，我一开始是作为 IC 加入的，然后我在一种需要它的组织结构中管理了一个团队几个月，现在我又全职回到 IC 工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I joined Anthropic as an IC because I was just really excited about the kind of work that there was to be done as an IC here, but also because I was feeling like I sort of want to be closer to the work, and I think this feels like a really important time to do it, before I ascend the corporate ranks.
zh: 我作为 IC 加入 Anthropic 是因为我对这里作为 IC 可以做的那种工作感到非常兴奋，但也因为我觉得我想更贴近工作，我认为在我攀登企业职级之前，这感觉像是一个非常重要的时候去做这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was having these questions and doubts about, is middle management, is that safe in the future?
zh: 而且我对这些问题有疑问和怀疑，中层管理，这在未来安全吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is the way that we're working actually, is this going to be a job that persists in the future?
zh: 我们的工作方式实际上，这是未来会持续存在的工作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or should I try something else and get my hands dirty kind of thing?
zh: 或者我应该尝试别的东西并亲自动手那种感觉？

speaker: Unknown
en: And to be totally fair, I actually love both sides of the coin.
zh: 完全公平地说，我实际上喜欢这枚硬币的两面。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love managing people.
zh: 我喜欢管理人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love setting up teams and being at that level, but I also just really love IC work.
zh: 我喜欢组建团队并处于那个层级，但我实际上也非常喜欢 IC 工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was sort of a reluctant manager when I did it, and I was like, "Okay, I'll do it."
zh: 当我做管理者时，我有点不情愿，我当时想，「好吧，我会做的。」

speaker: Unknown
en: So I love both sides of the coin pretty equally.
zh: 所以我几乎同样喜欢这枚硬币的两面。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think actually what being an IC across this past year has taught me, is that it actually just gave me a lot of skills that I don't think I would've gained if I was just managing throughout this year.
zh: 但我认为实际上，在过去的一年里作为一名 IC 教会了我的是，它实际上给了我很多技能，如果这一年我只是在管理，我觉得我不会获得这些技能。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like I mentioned, the design process has changed so, so much in this past year, and I feel like I've just picked up so many hard skills that I wouldn't have necessarily had the time to do if I was just managing a team.
zh: 就像我提到的，设计流程在过去的一年里发生了如此巨大的变化，我觉得我掌握了很多硬技能，如果我只是管理一个团队，我不一定有时间去做这些。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's actually the best thing it's afforded me.
zh: 所以这实际上是它给我的最好的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think at any point if I'm managing a team again, I think it will give me the empathy and understanding of how the design process has changed.
zh: 我认为在任何时候，如果我又管理一个团队，我认为它会给我对设计流程如何变化的同理心和理解。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's actually a really important thing right now because the teams are working so differently.
zh: 我认为这实际上是现在非常重要的事情，因为团队的工作方式非常不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's actually pretty hard to empathize if you are not working in that way, or you're not always testing all the tools and trying stuff.
zh: 我认为如果你不以这种方式工作，或者你总是不测试所有工具并尝试东西，实际上很难产生同理心。

speaker: Unknown
en: But yeah, it's an interesting time to be a designer.
zh: 是的，对于设计师来说这是一个有趣的时代。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if I had not worked in this environment, I don't know if I would've totally understood it or knew what to do or how to guide my teams.
zh: 如果我没有在这个环境中工作，我不知道我是否会完全理解它，或者知道该做什么或如何指导我的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's sort of what this year really gave me.
zh: 所以这就是这一年真正给我的东西。

speaker: Lenny
en: And so you were previously a director of design at Figma, right?
zh: 所以你之前是 Figma 的设计总监，对吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: How big was your team?
zh: 你的团队有多大？

speaker: Unknown
en: How large was your org, just to give people a reference?
zh: 你的组织有多大，只是给大家一个参考？

speaker: Unknown
en: At the max, I probably had, I think, 12, 15 designers or so, and I had a few managers as well.
zh: 最多的时候，我可能有，我想，12、15 个设计师左右，我也有几个经理。

speaker: Lenny
en: Cool. And then you went back to an IC?
zh: 酷。然后你回到了 IC？

speaker: Unknown
en: It was like an org.
zh: 就像一个组织。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you had the sense that middle management might not last.
zh: 所以你有中层管理可能不会持久的感觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your current feeling?
zh: 你现在的感觉是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you think design management is a thing that persists long-term, or do you think everyone turns into IC?
zh: 你认为设计管理是一件长期存在的事情，还是你认为每个人都会变成 IC？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think as long as there is a team of people, it helps to have somebody who is managing a team.
zh: 我认为只要有一群人，有一个人来管理团队是有帮助的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think there's real value in managers.
zh: 我认为管理者具有真正的价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: It depends what the shape of the manager is and what they actually do.
zh: 这取决于管理者的形态以及他们实际做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the way I think about what a helpful manager is these days, is somebody who is not just, I think pure people management like, oh, just somebody to set you up, help you in your career, have one-on-ones, make sure you're feeling good at work.
zh: 但我如今思考一个有帮助的管理者是什么样的，是不仅仅，我认为纯粹的人员管理比如，哦，只是某人来安排你，帮助你的职业生涯，进行一对一面谈，确保你在工作中感觉良好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that that is not a thing as much anymore, but I think somebody who can really function as giving the team direction, as well as doing some of the people management stuff, that tied together, I think is the future of what managing looks like, at least for now.
zh: 我认为那样的事情不再那么多了，但我认为真正能起到给团队指明方向作用的人，以及做一些人员管理的事情，这两点结合在一起，我认为是管理未来的样子，至少目前是。

speaker: Unknown
en: Somebody who can really engage with the team in terms of the work and giving direction there, as well as creating the environment for them to do their best work.
zh: 能够真正在工作方面与团队接触并给予指导的人，以及为他们创造最佳工作环境的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And do you see yourself going back into management long-term?
zh: 你看到自己在长期回到管理层吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I will.
zh: 我会的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I probably will.
zh: 我大概会的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think I really just love helping a team build the best product possible.
zh: 我认为我真的只是喜欢帮助团队构建尽可能好的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And my motto there is, whatever it takes.
zh: 我在那里的座右铭是，不惜一切代价。

speaker: Unknown
en: If it's somebody that, if the team needs somebody to give the team direction and set up the team and whatnot, that could be me.
zh: 如果团队需要某人来给团队方向并组建团队等等，那可能就是我。

speaker: Unknown
en: If the team just needs somebody to execute on it, that could be me as well.
zh: 如果团队只是需要某人来执行它，那也可能是我。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the advice I'm hearing for people in design that are especially managers, is you almost need to move back into IC in order to truly understand what is happening and how much it's changing, so that you can be a better manager.
zh: 所以我听到的给设计人员尤其是管理者的建议是，你几乎需要回到 IC 中去，才能真正理解正在发生什么以及它变化有多大，这样你才能成为更好的管理者。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think so.
zh: 我认为是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think traditionally, at least what I've seen, a lot of the engineering disciplines, when they hire EMs or even sometimes directors there, they actually make the EMs take a rotation for a few months and pick up a few tasks, and really understand how the technology works before they become a full-time manager.
zh: 我认为传统上，至少我所看到的，很多工程学科，当他们招聘工程经理甚至有时那里的总监时，他们实际上让工程经理轮岗几个月，接手一些任务，并真正理解技术是如何运作的，然后他们成为全职经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think design probably needs to do something similar too, where I think in the past design has been much more people management oriented.
zh: 我认为设计可能也需要做类似的事情，我认为在过去设计要更加以人为本的管理导向。

speaker: Unknown
en: What did you find yourself most rusty in when you went back to IC designer?
zh: 当你回到 IC 设计师时，你发现自己最生疏的是什么？

speaker: Jenny Wen
en: Actually doing crits, and just really-
zh: 实际上是做评审，以及真的——

speaker: Unknown
en: Getting criticized.
zh: 受到批评。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... Yeah, getting criticized.
zh: ……是的，受到批评。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're like, oh yeah, it is hard to get critical feedback and to hear it, and to hear it on such a regular basis, because that's the thing you have to do as a designer, is it's a pretty vulnerable exercise to share work and present it with your team, and then also just get a lot of critical feedback and take that all the time.
zh: 你就像，哦是的，很难获得批判性的反馈并听进去，并且如此定期地听进去，因为那是设计师必须做的事情，分享工作并与团队展示它是一个相当脆弱的练习，然后还要得到很多批判性的反馈并一直接受它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So currently you're leading design/IC designing on cowork.
zh: 所以目前你正在领导 cowork 的设计/IC 设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that right?
zh: 那是对的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So Boris, he was on the pod recently, talked about how there's a lot of debate about what cowork should be, and there's all these big ideas and he's like, "In the end, let's just make it like a terminal, basically, in the product, and just kind of a fancy terminal."
zh: 所以 Boris，他最近上了这个播客，谈到了关于 cowork 应该是什么有很多争论，有所有这些大想法，他说，“最后，让我们把它做成像一个终端，基本上，在产品里，只是某种花哨的终端。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything you could share about just the process of landing on where you landed for that experience of cowork?
zh: 关于最终确定那个 cowork 体验的过程，你有什么可以分享的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I have it here on my monitor, by the way, looking at it.
zh: 顺便说一下，我的显示器上就有它，我正在看着它。

speaker: Unknown
en: With Cowork specifically, we have had a bunch of different prototypes internally of what that could look like.
zh: 对于 Cowork 具体来说，我们在内部有很多关于它可能是什么样子的不同原型。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's one of those things where we tried a lot of things, and then I think we weren't really sure when it was actually going to be ready to ship.
zh: 这是那种事情之一，我们尝试了很多东西，然后我们并不确定它何时真正准备好发布。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it was sort of everything all at once.
zh: 然后它是一次性所有的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were like, "Okay, we're going to ship it soon."
zh: 我们就像，“好吧，我们要很快发布它。”

speaker: Lenny
en: I think it was 10 days, 10 days of building.
zh: 我想是 10 天，10 天的构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it was definitely longer than that overall.
zh: 是的，总的来说肯定比那要长。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was 10 days to get it from what we had internally to something that we were ready to ship externally.
zh: 从我们在内部拥有的东西到我们准备好对外发布的东西，花了 10 天。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we'd been building it for a while, but we weren't really sure about the actual form it was going to take.
zh: 所以我们已经构建了一段时间，但我们并不确定它实际会采取的形式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the way it got there is actually, there was a lot of different other explorations that we had internally on top of different agent harnesses and whatnot.
zh: 所以它到达那里的方式实际上是，我们在内部有很多不同的其他探索，基于不同的智能体 harness 等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we just had prototyped little parts of the different interactions that ended up in cowork.
zh: 我们只是原型化了最终进入 cowork 的不同交互的一小部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: So things like when Claude gives you a to do list, we tried a bunch of different form factors for that.
zh: 所以像当 Claude 给你一个待办事项清单时，我们尝试了一堆不同的形式因素。

speaker: Unknown
en: We tried a bunch of different form factors for the way it presents you different multiple choice questions.
zh: 我们尝试了一堆不同的形式因素，用于它向你展示不同的多项选择题的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: We tried a bunch of different ways to teach people what the use cases are and whatnot.
zh: 我们尝试了一堆不同的方式来教人们用例是什么等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't know if we landed on the best form factor ever, but essentially it was stuff that was already working internally that people liked, that we just thought we were going to get some more signal on by releasing it.
zh: 我不确定我们是否确立了有史以来最好的形式因素，但本质上它是已经在内部起作用的东西，人们喜欢的，我们只是认为通过发布它会得到更多的信号。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think forcing ourselves to release it within that 10 days that we did, it was just sort of like, whatever we had, let's put it out there and then let's go out there and iterate from there, which is what we're doing.
zh: 所以我认为强迫我们在那 10 天内发布它，就像，无论我们有什么，让我们把它放在那里，然后让我们从那里开始迭代，这就是我们正在做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it blew up the internet when you launched it, so it worked out.
zh: 当你们发布它时，它在互联网上爆火了，所以很成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there a feature of Cowork today that you're either most proud of, or just can't wait to fix and improve?
zh: Cowork 今天有没有哪个功能是你最自豪的，或者只是迫不及待地想要修复和改进的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Honestly, I think I'm just most proud of us actually just shipping it, to be honest, and putting it out there.
zh: 坦白说，我觉得我最自豪的其实是我们真的把它做出来了，老实说，并且把它发布了出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: And yeah, I don't know if there's one specific thing yet, because I think when you work on something and you work at so long, especially as a designer, you're like, I don't know.
zh: 是的，我不知道是否有一个具体的亮点，因为我觉得当你在某件事上工作，并且工作了这么久，尤其是作为一名设计师，你会像，我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: All I can do is see flaws in it, but I think there's a lot of stuff that I'm excited about.
zh: 我只能看到它的缺陷，但我觉得有很多东西我很兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have been iterating, especially on the homepage, and to make that something where it feels more like, "Hey, these are tasks you can give Claude and the tasks that Claude are working on. "
zh: 我们一直在迭代，尤其是在主页上，让它变成某种感觉更像“嘿，这些是你可以交给 Claude 的任务，以及 Claude 正在处理的任务”的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that actually should be rolling out.
zh: 所以这实际上应该正在推出。

speaker: Unknown
en: It might already be rolled out by the time this comes out.
zh: 在这个发布的时候，它可能已经推出了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I see this little randomizer thing, where you click it and gives you all these different ideas.
zh: 我看到这个小随机化器的东西，你点击它，它会给你所有这些不同的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, yeah.
zh: 是的，是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then so when you actually start to work with Claude on stuff, it feels more like a to do list.
zh: 所以当你真正开始和 Claude 一起处理事情时，它感觉更像是一个待办事项清单。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels more like these are things Claude's working on, these are things that Claude needs your attention on.
zh: 它感觉更像这些是 Claude 正在处理的事情，这些是 Claude 需要你关注的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there's an opportunity here to make it feel much more like this shared to do list between you and Claude.
zh: 我认为这里有一个机会，让它感觉更像是一个你和 Claude 之间共享的待办事项清单。

speaker: Unknown
en: So excited to iterate on that.
zh: 所以很兴奋能继续迭代那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I'm also excited to think more about what is the actual true form factor of this?
zh: 然后我也很兴奋能更多地思考真正的实际形态因素是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it stuck in this screen always, or how does this reach out to the different surfaces that it's working with?
zh: 它是永远卡在这个屏幕里，还是它如何延伸到它正在处理的不同表面？

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that you shared that it wasn't just 10 days to do this thing.
zh: 我很高兴你分享了做这件事不仅仅花了 10 天。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's these numbers that people throw out there, "We built it in 10 days."
zh: 有些数字人们会抛出来，“我们在 10 天内构建了它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And your point is there was time spent thinking about what direction it should go, and prototyping, mocking, trying stuff.
zh: 你的观点是，有时间花在思考它应该朝什么方向发展，以及制作原型、模型、尝试东西上。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it's like, okay, now we know what we want it to be.
zh: 然后就像，好的，现在我们知道我们想要它成为什么样子了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's build it and ship it.
zh: 让我们构建它并发布它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think for some reason that became the viral thing that got taken away from all of the cowork announcements, is that it only took 10 days, but I think there have just been so many different explorations, and people that have worked on different pieces of cowork, that it was not just 10 days, and there was a lot of different people involved.
zh: 我觉得出于某种原因，这成了从所有 cowork 公告中被带走的热点，就是它只花了 10 天，但我认为只是有太多不同的探索，以及在不同 cowork 部件上工作的人，所以它不仅仅是 10 天，并且涉及到很多不同的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's one of those things where it's like the idea kept coming back, and it's never the right moment or there's different variations of it, and then all of a sudden it's the right moment and it feels like, oh, so obvious all along, but there was a long, long journey to get there.
zh: 这是那种事情之一，就像这个想法不断回来，并且从来都不是合适的时刻，或者它有不同的变体，然后突然间它是合适的时刻，并且感觉像，哦，一直以来都很明显，但是有一条很长的旅程才到达那里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, for people that don't know much about cowork, the way I think about it, it's like Claude with hands, or to do stuff on your computer.
zh: 顺便说一下，对于那些不太了解 cowork 的人，我对它的思考方式，它是 like 有手的 Claude，或者在你的电脑上做事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: How would you describe it, just in a sentence or two?
zh: 你会怎么描述它，只用一两句话？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a good description.
zh: 那是一个很好的描述。

speaker: Unknown
en: I actually haven't heard that, but I like that.
zh: 我实际上没听过那个，但我喜欢那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I might use it more often as Claude with hands.
zh: 我可能会更经常地使用它 as 有手的 Claude。

speaker: Unknown
en: I also think about it as it's like Claude, but Claude is really good at taking all your garbage and then turning it into something nice.
zh: 我也把它思考为它是 like Claude，但 Claude 非常擅长拿走你所有的垃圾，然后把它变成美好的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one of my favorite, any sort of use case that I really like out of cowork, is just giving it a folder of my stuff, and it doesn't really matter what's in that folder, but I'm able to extract something good out of it.
zh: 我认为我最喜欢的事情之一，任何我真的喜欢的来自 cowork 的用例，就是给它一个我东西的文件夹，并且那个文件夹里是什么并不重要，但我能够从中提取一些好的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've done that many times.
zh: 我做过那个很多次。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Coming back to managing and being a manager and the role of a designer, I'm going to talk about hiring for a little bit.
zh: 回到管理和作为一名经理以及设计师的角色，我要谈谈招聘一会儿。

speaker: Unknown
en: So seeing how much is changing in the role of a designer, what do you look for that's maybe new?
zh: 所以看到设计师的角色中正在发生多大的变化，你寻找什么可能是新的东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you now look for when you're hiring designers, that you think is really important for them to be successful in this new world?
zh: 你现在在招聘设计师时寻找什么，你认为对他们在这个新世界中取得成功真的很重要？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I do think working specifically in the kind of environment that I do, there's just a sense of resilience and roll with it, kind of thing, that I think is really important because so much is changing around us and you have to be really willing to adapt, to try out new methods, to try out new tools and learn stuff, as opposed to just be stuck in the old processes and the old ways.
zh: 嗯，我确实认为工作在我工作的特定类型的环境中，有一种韧性和随波逐流的感觉，那种事情，我认为真的很重要，因为我们周围有太多东西在变化，你必须真的愿意适应，尝试新方法，尝试新工具并学习东西，而不是只是卡在旧的流程和旧的方式中。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then I think about also, there's probably three archetypes of folks that are really interesting to me right now.
zh: 然后我也思考，大概有三种原型的人，现在对我来说真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think these folks were already interesting to me before, but I feel like in this era feels especially important.
zh: 我认为这些人以前对我来说已经很有趣了，但我觉得在这个时代感觉特别重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the first one I would call is strong generalists.
zh: 所以我会叫的第一个是强大的通才。

speaker: Unknown
en: So not just regular generalists where they're kind of good at a lot of things, but people that are almost block shaped, in that T-shaped framework, where it's like they're really good at a few core skills, like 80th percentile good.
zh: 所以不只是普通的通才，他们对很多事情都还不错，而是那些几乎是块状的人，在那个 T 型框架中，就像他们在几个核心技能上真的很好，像 80 百分位数的好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think this is pretty rare and hard to hire for, to be honest, but I like this because the design role we've already seen is stretching and spanning.
zh: 我认为这相当罕见并且很难招聘，老实说，但我喜欢这个，因为我们已经看到设计师角色正在伸展和跨越。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're all becoming more PM shape, we're all becoming engineering shaped.
zh: 我们都正在变得更像 PM 形状，我们都正在变得更像工程形状。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if you already have strong skills in a few different buckets, it's really easy for you to flex around and expand your role.
zh: 所以如果你已经在几个不同的桶中有强大的技能，对你来说很容易灵活变动并扩展你的角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's really exciting to me.
zh: 所以这对我来说真的令人兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just somebody who is really good at a bunch of things, again, a huge ask.
zh: 它只是一个真的擅长很多事情的人，同样，一个巨大的要求。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the other person that's really exciting to me, is in that T-shaped framework, a deep specialist, someone who is T-shaped, but the tip of the T probably goes down farther than most other people.
zh: 然后另一个对我来说真的令人兴奋的人，是在那个 T 型框架中，一个深度专家，一个 T 型的人，但 T 的尖端可能比大多数其他人都要往下深得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: So folks that are maybe the top 10% in the industry and whatnot.
zh: 所以那些可能是行业前 10% 的人之类的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, super hard to find.
zh: 同样，超级难找到。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I feel very lucky that working at some of these places, folks like these, you could sort of afford to hire them and actually bring them on board.
zh: 我感到很幸运，在这些地方工作，像这样的人，你实际上负担得起雇佣他们并真正把他们带上船。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then my last one is probably the one that I think we're all overlooking, which is what I call the craft new grad.
zh: 然后我的最后一个可能是我认为我们都忽视的那个，我称之为工艺新手。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just somebody who's early career and feels, like, wise and experienced beyond their years, but is also just very humble and very eager to learn.
zh: 它只是一个处于职业生涯早期的人，并且感觉，像，智慧和经验超越了他们的岁月，但也只是非常谦逊并且非常渴望学习。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think this person is really interesting right now because I think most companies are just hiring senior talent, folks that have done things before, are super experienced, but given how much the roles are changing and what we're expected to do is changing, I think having somebody who almost has a blank slate, and is just a really quick learner and is really eager to learn new tactics and stuff like that, and doesn't have all these baked in processes and rituals in their mind, that's super valuable.
zh: 我认为这个人现在真的很有趣，因为我认为大多数公司只是招聘高级人才，那些以前做过事情的人，非常有经验的人，但考虑到角色的变化程度以及我们要做的事情的变化程度，我认为拥有一个几乎是白纸一张的人，是一个非常快速的学习者，并且非常渴望学习新战术和类似的东西，并且脑海中没有所有这些既定的流程和仪式，这是非常有价值的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think those are the folks that I think a lot of us are just overlooking, but I'm really excited about.
zh: 所以我认为这些人是我们要很多人只是忽略了，但我真的很兴奋的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is awesome.
zh: 这太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the deep T shape, what's an example of someone in design that has a ... What's a skill that they're really good at?
zh: 关于深 T 型，在设计中有一个人的例子是什么……他们真正擅长的技能是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes there's designers who are just really technical in a way where they could be 50% of ... they're basically a software engineer.
zh: 有时有些设计师在技术方面真的很强，在某种意义上他们可以是 50% 的……他们基本上就是软件工程师。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's really interesting, especially because right now a lot of it is, at least for us, it's like you're working directly with the model, so it helps when you have just deep engineering expertise.
zh: 这真的很有趣，特别是因为现在很多时候，至少对我们来说，就像你直接与模型一起工作，所以当你拥有深厚的工程专业知识时会有帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: But another deep specialist T is just maybe they're just really good at visual design or just designing icons or something, where things like that, given that anybody can make anything, having that deep specialist slant feels like, oh yeah, they can really help differentiate the things that we're building.
zh: 但另一个深度的专家型 T 可能只是他们非常擅长视觉设计或者只是设计图标之类的东西，鉴于现在任何人都可以做任何事情，拥有那种深度专家的倾向感觉像是，哦是的，他们真的可以帮助区分我们正在构建的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's block shape.
zh: 然后还有块状型。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had Mark Andrews on the podcast, we kind of called it the F shape or E shape, where there's multiple T things, sideways F, sideways E, I guess.
zh: 我们请 Mark Andrews 上过播客，我们有点称之为 F 型或 E 型，那里有多个 T 的东西，侧向的 F，侧向的 E，我想。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that what you're describing, where there's many things you're really, really good at?
zh: 这就是你描述的吗，有很多你非常、非常擅长的事情？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And basically, I don't know, if you almost had their skillset, it would look like a block instead of a T.
zh: 基本上，我不知道，如果你几乎拥有他们的技能组合，它看起来会像一块而不是一个 T。

speaker: Lenny
en: Because there's so many skills that the T is spread out.
zh: 因为有太多的技能，T 被分散开了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Lenny
en: Okay. And the crack new grad. So this is just someone that is eager, open-minded, gritty, very smart, I imagine is a big part of it.
zh: 好的。还有那个出色的新毕业生。所以这只是一个渴望的、思想开放的、坚毅的、非常聪明的人，我想这是很大的一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: If someone's a young designer trying to break in, trying to be successful, what would your advice be to them to help them have a shot at joining Anthropic, for example?
zh: 如果有人是一个试图进入这个行业、试图取得成功的年轻设计师，你会给他们什么建议来帮助他们有机会加入 Anthropic，例如？

speaker: Unknown
en: I would just say just build a bunch of stuff, try a bunch of stuff out, build actual things.
zh: 我只想说就是构建一堆东西，尝试一堆东西，构建实际的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that can feel ... I don't really know what the state of design education or education is these days, but at least from back when I was in school, everything was very theoretical, and here, we're going to teach you some approaches and whatnot.
zh: 我认为这感觉可能……我不知道设计教育或教育的现状这些天是怎样的，但至少从当年我在学校的时候起，一切都是非常理论化的，在这里，我们将教你一些方法之类的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the best crack new grad folks I know are just people who just use the technology, build actual things, don't feel limited by how little experience they might have.
zh: 但我所知道的最好的出色新毕业生只是那些使用技术、构建实际东西、不要被他们可能拥有的很少经验所限制的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that sometimes they're actually unburdened by that, because we have expectations of ourselves after being in the industry for so long, but they actually don't and they sort of feel like anything is possible.
zh: 我认为有时他们实际上因此没有负担，因为我们在进入行业这么久后对自己有期望，但他们实际上没有，他们感觉什么都是可能的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so just building a bunch of stuff and sharing it with people, and finding a community of folks that also do that.
zh: 所以只是构建一堆东西并与人们分享，并找到一个也这样做的社区。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think my one call it here too, is I went to a school that started something called Socratica a few years after, actually a while after I graduated.
zh: 我认为我这里的一个号召也是，我去了一所学校，在我毕业几年后，实际上是很久之后，开始了 something called Socratica。

speaker: Unknown
en: And basically their whole thing is building stuff and showcasing it almost like a science project.
zh: 基本上他们的全部事情就是构建东西并几乎像科学项目一样展示它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there's just been a really cool movement there of folks who just build things and do things.
zh: 我认为那里有一个非常酷的运动，那些只是构建东西和做事情的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: For example, somebody built this Claude robot project, this was a few years ago too, where they were just assembling robots that were running on Claude, and then somebody else did something where she just wanted to put Googly eyes on a bus in Boston or something.
zh: 例如，有人构建了这个 Claude 机器人项目，这也是几年前的事了，他们只是组装运行在 Claude 上的机器人，然后其他人做了某件事，她只是想在波士顿的公共汽车上贴上 Googly eyes 或类似的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's just a sense of both agency in terms of, yeah, we can just do stuff, but then also this community where people were just trying and building things and sharing things with each other.
zh: 有一种主见感，是的，我们可以做事情，但也有这个社区，人们只是尝试和构建东西并彼此分享东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So whatever that looks like, given the school that someone's from or graduating from, doing that kind of stuff is the stuff that will make people stand out.
zh: 所以无论那看起来像什么，考虑到某人来自或即将毕业的学校，做那种事情是会让人们脱颖而出的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: For current designers that are [inaudible 00:52:54] career, [inaudible 00:52:55] senior, do you think that you need to get technical and learn to code, at least build, or do you think you could be really successful and just not lean into that and get better at other stuff?
zh: 对于目前处于职业生涯 [听不清 00:52:54]、[听不清 00:52:55] 高级的设计师，你认为你需要变得技术化并学习编写代码，至少构建，还是认为你真的可以成功，只是不倾向于此并在其他方面变得更好？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it definitely helps to maybe not learn how to code so much that you're building something from scratch, but it does feel like more and more of the designer's vocabulary right now is implementing some stuff.
zh: 我认为这肯定有帮助，也许不需要学习如何编写那么多代码以至于你从零开始构建东西，但这感觉确实，越来越多的设计师的词汇表现在是实现一些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wonder though, as the models, both the models and the products get better, I mean, we probably will continue to move up the abstraction layers and you won't have to actually know how each single line of code will work.
zh: 我想知道，随着模型，包括模型和产品变得更好，我的意思是，我们可能会继续在抽象层上向上移动，你不必真正知道每一行代码是如何工作的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think what I would say is start to bring that into your toolkit, the coding tools, whether or not it's like you're actually becoming technical, I think any designer should just be really aware, and know how to use the tools that are at hand, as opposed to maybe learning and going off and learning React or et cetera, et cetera.
zh: 但我想我会说的是开始将其带入你的工具包，编码工具，无论你是否正在变得技术化，我认为任何设计师都应该只是真正意识到，并知道如何使用手头的工具，而不是去学习并离开去学习 React 或等等等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: How good of a designer is Claude, would you say, or Claude Code, however you want to describe ... Would you hire Claude as a designer or is it not there yet?
zh: Claude 作为一名设计师有多好，你会说，或者 Claude Code，无论你想怎么描述……你会雇佣 Claude 作为设计师还是还没到那个程度？

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think Claude is there yet.
zh: 我认为 Claude 还没到那个程度。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think Claude is there yet in terms of a designer you would hire.
zh: 我认为 Claude 还没到你会雇佣的设计师的程度。

en: I think it is not yet the strong generalist or the deep specialist or the crack new grad.
zh: 我认为它还不是那个强大的通才或深度专家或出色的新毕业生。

speaker: Unknown

zh: 我认为它在第一遍上相当不错，并且在向你展示一堆不同的想法，但那里的东西没有什么感觉特别和可雇佣的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is good news for designers for now.
zh: 这对设计师来说目前是个好消息。

speaker: Unknown
en: It sucks at this for now.

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm so curious to see how good it could get at this.
zh: 我真的很好奇它在这方面的表现能有多好。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's such a big open question, is can it pump out amazing novel, unique creative experiences, or it's just never going to be that good as a human designer?
zh: 这是一个巨大的开放性问题，它能否产出惊人的新颖的、独特的创造性体验，还是它永远不会像人类设计师那样好？

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, it's gotten a lot better in the last year or so, even.
zh: 我的意思是，它在过去一年左右已经好多了。

speaker: Unknown
