en: There's a couple management, I don't know, rituals or takes you have that I've heard from folks that you work with, that I want to touch on.
zh: 有几个管理，我不知道，仪式或观点，我从与你共事的人那里听到的，我想谈谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is that you have this hot take that low leverage time for managers is just not a thing, that there's a lot of benefit you can get out of things that people consider low leverage.
zh: 一是你有一个激进观点，即经理的低杠杆时间根本不存在，你可以从人们认为低杠杆的事情中获得很多好处。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you talk about that?
zh: 你能谈谈这个吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I remember first becoming a manager, and I think one of the pieces of advice that I either got from a course or a book or something, is like, yeah, "Now that you're a manager, you have to really prioritize your time and categorize your work."
zh: 我记得刚开始做经理的时候，我想我从课程或书或什么地方得到的一条建议是，“既然你是经理了，你必须真正确定你的时间优先级并对你的工作进行分类。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And there was this two by two of ... I don't remember what it was in it, but you essentially say, "Oh, these are the things that only I can do.
zh: 那里有一个二乘二矩阵……我不记得里面有什么了，但你基本上是说，“哦，这些是只有我能做的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: These are things anybody else can do, and everything else, it's low leverage and you shouldn't do that anymore."
zh: 这些是其他人都能做的事，其他所有事情都是低杠杆的，你不应该再那样做了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And a lot of the low leverage things were just things that are really nitpicky in the weeds, or just literally, yes, probably somebody else could do those tasks.
zh: 很多低杠杆的事情只是那些真正在细节上吹毛求疵的事情，或者字面上是的，也许其他人可以完成那些任务。

speaker: Unknown
en: But when I think about leaders and managers that I respect the most, I actually think some of their best traits is that they choose low leverage tasks that they take on themselves, and that actually ends up being actually a very high leverage thing, because it's them who's doing it.
zh: 但当我想到我最尊敬的领导者和经理时，我实际上认为他们最好的特质之一是他们选择自己承担的低杠杆任务，而这最终实际上是一件非常高杠杆的事情，因为是他们在做。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one example is whenever you have senior leaders who just test the shit out of the product, and they're just so in tune with it, and they dog food it, they repro the bugs, they spend a bunch of time with engineers sharing the logs and nitpicking and stuff like that.
zh: 所以一个例子是，每当你有高级领导者对产品进行疯狂的测试，他们非常了解产品，他们试用产品，他们复现错误，他们花大量时间与工程师分享日志和吹毛求疵等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that ends up being super actually high leverage, even though it's a lot of time, of nitty-gritty time, because it creates this familiarity with the product, which I think is really good.
zh: 我认为这最终实际上是超高杠杆的，尽管这需要花费大量的细致时间，因为它创造了这种对产品的熟悉度，我认为这非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: It also creates this vibe where it's like, oh yeah, this senior leader really cares deeply and they actually know the ins and out of the product, and they're rolling up their sleeves and they're giving this feedback and working with the team on it.
zh: 它还创造了一种氛围，就像哦耶，这位高级领导者真的很在意，而且他们实际上了解产品的里里外外，他们卷起袖子提供反馈并与团队合作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think similarly to what I've seen is when a senior leader is able to fix a bug now.
zh: 我认为我看到的类似情况是，当一位高级领导者能够立即修复一个错误时。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think I've actually seen Mike Krieger before put in PRs himself, and it's really nice because it's like, okay, cool, we're all on this team together and nothing is below this person.
zh: 我想我以前确实看到 Mike Krieger 自己提交 PR，这真的很好，就像，好吧，酷，我们都在这个团队里，没有什么事情是在这个人之下的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think another thing that I love that's a little bit more cultural, is when somebody goes out of the way to make somebody's anniversary card or something, and vibe code them something super nice, or make them something, a super nice card, because I think it just shows that it's like an EA or somebody can put together the card, but this leader is just somebody who cares so much about their team that they put in the effort.
zh: 我认为我喜欢的另一件更具文化色彩的事情是，当有人特意为某人制作周年纪念卡或其他东西，并为他们写一些非常好的 vibe code，或者为他们制作一些东西，一张非常好的卡片，因为我认为这只是表明，虽然 EA 或某人可以制作这张卡片，但这领导者只是一个非常关心团队的人，所以他们付出了努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's something I try to embody, is choosing the seemingly low leverage tasks that are worth my time.
zh: 所以这就是我试图体现的，选择那些值得我花时间的看似低杠杆的任务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that is so interesting.
zh: 是的，这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What you're saying there is, in a sense, the low leverage stuff is the stuff that often has the most impact because your reports wouldn't expect you to spend time on this thing, and the low leverage ends up being high leverage.
zh: 你在这里说的在某种意义上是，低杠杆的东西通常是具有最大影响力的东西，因为你的下属不会期望你花时间在这上面，而低杠杆最终变成了高杠杆。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's what makes your style of leadership stand out, or feel special to a certain person.
zh: 我认为这也是你的领导风格脱颖而出的原因，或者让某个人感觉特别。

speaker: Unknown
en: Amazing.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Another, I don't know, ritual and way of running teams that I heard about you, is you encourage team members roasting each other, which on the surface doesn't sound like a wonderful environment, but on the other hand, I hear constantly that the teams that you've built are just the happiest, the highest performing teams.
zh: 另一个，我不知道，我听说你的仪式和团队运作方式，是你鼓励团队成员互相吐槽，这表面上听起来不是一个美好的环境，但另一方面，我不断听到你建立的团队是最快乐、绩效最高的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about, I guess, this idea of roasting and encouraging that, and just what you've learned about building awesome teams.
zh: 谈谈我想，这个吐槽和鼓励它的想法，以及你关于建立棒极了的团队学到了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think it's not that I'm like, "Yo, you should roast each other."
zh: 是的，我认为不是我就像，“哟，你们应该互相吐槽。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm not forcing it in that way or anything, but when I think about the psychological safety, and teams and people that just get along with each other, when you think about your friends, you're always willing to push the boundaries a little bit and roast them.
zh: 我不是以那种方式强迫它或任何事情，但当我想到心理安全感，以及团队和只是相处得很好的人时，当你想到你的朋友时，你总是愿意稍微推一下界限并吐槽他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're roasting your friends a lot, but you actually might not be roasting your coworkers a lot, because it's all just about comfort and safety.
zh: 你经常吐槽你的朋友，但你实际上可能不会经常吐槽你的同事，因为这完全是关于舒适和安全感。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's not that I'm like, "Oh, I want my teams to roast each other."
zh: 所以不是我就像，“噢，我希望我的团队互相吐槽。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think it can be a really good sign when the people on your team feel comfortable just poking fun at each other a little bit.
zh: 但我认为当你的团队的人感到舒适，只是稍微互相取笑时，这可能是一个非常好的迹象。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that also can be a good sign when folks also feel the same way about you as a leader, where it's like there's just an element of they don't fear you as much, and they feel like there's a sense of safety where if they say something, they're not going to get fired.
zh: 我认为这也可以是一个非常好的迹象，当人们对你作为领导者也有同样的感觉时，就像有一种元素是他们不那么害怕你，并且他们感觉有一种安全感，如果他们说什么，他们不会被解雇。

speaker: Unknown
en: So an example of this is, with my last team, I feel like they would make fun of things that I would say at crits sometimes, certain phrases I would say.
zh: 所以一个例子是，对于我上一个团队，我觉得他们有时会嘲笑我在评论时说的话，我会说的某些短语。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's an example of that?
zh: 那有什么例子吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, I would always be like, "Okay, what are next steps, and how do we follow up on this?"
zh: 噢，我总是会像，“好吧，下一步是什么，我们如何跟进这个？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they'd be like, "Okay, what are next steps?"
zh: 然后他们会像，“好吧，下一步是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And they would sort of channel me in that way.
zh: 他们会以那种方式模仿我。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I just think it shows a level of like, okay, these people are not necessarily afraid of me, they know that they trust me, they can trust me, and then they sort of know enough about each other, and me personally and our personal lives, to be able to know where those boundaries are.
zh: 是的，我只是认为它显示了一种水平，就像，好吧，这些人并不一定害怕我，他们知道他们信任我，他们可以信任我，然后他们彼此了解足够多，也了解我个人和我们的个人生活，能够知道那些界限在哪里。

speaker: Unknown
en: But at the same time, I think the thing that you err into in that territory, is as a manager, are you friends with your reports?
zh: 但同时，我认为你在那个领域容易犯的错误是，作为经理，你是你的下属的朋友吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is I think a thing people tell you not to do.
zh: 这是人们告诉不要做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the way I think about balancing this out is you have to create this baseline of psychological safety, and people feel comfortable both with each other and with you, but you also have to make sure that they know that you have really high standards.
zh: 所以我认为平衡这一点的方法是，你必须创造这种心理安全感的基线，人们彼此和你之间都感到舒适，但你也要确保他们知道你有非常高的标准。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think these two things can feel like they're at tension, but I think they work really well together, because it's like once you have that psychological safety, you have people trusting each other and you, applying the high standards actually I think becomes potentially easier because you can do it without fear, I think.
zh: 我认为这两件事可能感觉它们很紧张，但我认为它们一起工作得很好，因为就像一旦你有了那种心理安全感，你有人互相信任和你，应用高标准实际上我认为可能变得更容易，因为我认为你可以毫无恐惧地做到。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I sort of think about this from the approach of being a tough parent a little bit.
zh: 我从稍微做一个严厉的父母的方法来考虑这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "Oh yeah, my team, I work with them in a way where they know I'm always going to be there and I'm not just going to fire them on a whim or something.
zh: 就像，“噢耶，我的团队，我以一种方式与他们合作，他们知道我总是在那里，我不会只是一时兴起解雇他们或什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But at the same time, they also know that I want the best for them, and that I have high standards, and that I'm working with them to make the best work possible."
zh: 但同时，他们也知道我希望他们得到最好的，并且我有很高的标准，并且我正在与他们一起创造最好的工作。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so yeah, that's the balance I think you just strike is like, can you create this environment of one where your team feels comfortable roasting you, but at the same time they know they have to be doing great work, and they will do great work with you.
zh: 所以是的，我认为你只是达到的这种平衡是，你能否创造这样一种环境，你的团队感到舒适吐槽你，但同时他们知道他们必须做伟大的工作，并且他们将与你一起做伟大的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is awesome advice.
zh: 这是非常棒的建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting how often this just management style comes back, or management, good management, it comes back, reminds me of, what was it, radical candor, just this combination of caring deeply and challenging directly.
zh: 有趣的是，这种管理风格经常回归，或者好的管理，它回归，让我想起了，是什么，激进的坦诚，只是这种深切关怀和直接挑战的结合。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's kind of what I hear here, is just make sure people know that you care deeply about them, but also be very direct and have high standards.
zh: 我在这里听到的就是这样，只是确保人们知道你深切关心他们，但也要非常直接并有高标准。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's so interesting.
zh: 这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe a final question.
zh: 也许最后一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm always looking for interesting frameworks and methods and processes that people have found useful in their work.
zh: 我总是在寻找人们在其工作中发现有用的有趣框架和方法及流程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I hear you're a big fan of something called the legibility framework.
zh: 我听说你是某种被称为可读性框架的忠实粉丝。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about this, talk about how you use it, why it's so valuable.
zh: 谈谈这个，谈谈你是如何使用它的，以及它为什么如此有价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: This framework, I think I saw it on Twitter, maybe last year or something, and it was Evan Tana, who's a partner at SPC.
zh: 这个框架，我想我是在 Twitter 上看到的，也许是去年左右，那是 Evan Tana，SPC 的一位合伙人。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's a VC.
zh: 他是一位 VC。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it basically is this two by two.
zh: 所以它基本上是一个二乘二的矩阵。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think it got so much attention, but once I started seeing it, I actually couldn't stop thinking about it.
zh: 我认为它没有得到太多的关注，但一旦我开始看到它，我就真的无法停止思考它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So on the two by two, he basically has founders.
zh: 所以在这个二乘二的矩阵中，他基本上列出了创始人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Founders can be either illegible or legible, and then ideas can either be illegible or legible.
zh: 创始人可能是不可读的或可读的，然后想法也可能是不可读的或可读的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And basically he was saying that, "Okay, if both the founder and idea is super legible, the idea is probably not that novel, and somebody's already like, they're already going to implement it or do it and you're actually not finding something new."
zh: 基本上他在说，“好吧，如果创始人和想法都是超级可读的，那么这个想法可能不那么新颖，已经有人像那样要去实施它或做它了，而你实际上没有发现什么新东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But then where it gets really interesting is where the idea itself is illegible.
zh: 但真正有趣的地方在于想法本身是不可读的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by illegible, he means, "Oh, it's sort of really on the frontier, people might not get it yet," or the way it's being told, it's not being told in the way that makes the most sense to people.
zh: 关于不可读，他的意思是，“哦，它有点处于前沿，人们可能还没理解它，”或者它的讲述方式，不是以最能让人理解的方式讲述的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think this is obviously a good way for a VC to operate, because you're trying to look for the opportunities that people don't see and put them out there in the world.
zh: 我认为这显然是 VC 运作的好方法，因为你正在寻找人们没有看到的机会，并将它们推向世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I also think that part of the role of the designer, at least, at least at a frontier lab at Anthropic, is spotting the ideas that are illegible, and trying to understand what's there, and how to take that and transform it, whether it's through storytelling, or whether it's through the actual UX and the form factor, and put it out there.
zh: 但我也认为，设计师的部分角色，至少在 Anthropic 这样的前沿实验室，是发现那些不可读的想法，并尝试理解那里有什么，以及如何获取它并转化它，无论是通过叙事，还是通过实际的 UX 和形式因素，并将其推向世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think when I mentioned going through Slack and looking at all the stuff that people are making, that's kind of what I'm doing.
zh: 我想当我提到浏览 Slack 并查看人们制作的所有东西时，我就是在做这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm trying to see, oh yeah, what are the ideas that there's some energy there around, but might not make sense yet, that are worth me thinking about more in my work?
zh: 我试图看到，哦，是的，有哪些想法周围有一些能量，但可能还不合理，值得我在工作中多思考？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's one good example, actually, that ties to cowork where there was this internal prototype that we called Claude Studio that I think somebody built partway through last year, and it essentially was just this really kind of dense, powerful interface that was built on top of some agentic harness.
zh: 实际上有一个很好的例子，它与 Cowork 有关，有一个我们称为 Claude Studio 的内部原型，我想是有人在去年中途构建的，它基本上只是一个非常密集、强大的界面，是构建在某些智能体 harness 之上的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It might've been Claude Code at that point in time too.
zh: 在那个时间点它可能也是 Claude Code。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it had all these displays where it was showing you all this knowledge and all these skills and things Claude was doing, and previewing its outputs.
zh: 它有所有这些显示，向你展示所有这些知识和所有这些技能以及 Claude 正在做的事情，并预览它的输出。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think, to a designer, I looked at it and I was like, "I don't know what's going on.
zh: 我认为，对于一个设计师来说，我看着它，我心想，“我不知道发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't really get it."
zh: 我不太明白。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But then I sort of saw the folks in research, the folks building it and just folks internally, there's just a lot of energy around it.
zh: 但后来我看到研究部门的人，构建它的人以及内部的人，那里只是有很多能量。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was like, "Cool, I think there's something here, but I just don't understand it yet."
zh: 我心想，“酷，我觉得这里有东西，但我只是还不理解它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that really was an example of an illegible idea.
zh: 我认为这确实是一个不可读想法的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And ultimately what came from it was the skills framework and the markdown files that instruct Claude on how to do something.
zh: 最终从中产生的是技能框架和指导 Claude 如何做某事的 Markdown 文件。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that came out of that specific prototype.
zh: 所以那是从那个特定原型中出来的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That was not something I was directly involved in, and that was more of, the folks working this prototype extracted that out of it.
zh: 那不是我直接参与的，更多的是，从事这个原型的人从中提取了那个东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: But when it did come to work on Claude cowork and I was thinking about, oh yeah, what is the form factor for those things?
zh: 但是当确实要开始在 Claude Cowork 上工作时，我在思考，哦，是的，这些东西的形式因素是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Seeing that prototype and seeing the kinds of information that people found really helpful, seeing Claude's plan and to-dos, seeing Claude's context and the files that it was going through, those kinds of things are things that I ended up pulling out of that prototype into Claude cowork.
zh: 看到那个原型，看到人们觉得真的有用的那种信息，看到 Claude 的计划和待办事项，看到 Claude 的上下文和它正在浏览的文件，这些类型的事情是我最终从那个原型中提取到 Claude Cowork 中的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yeah, I think about how can designers almost be more like VCs in this way, internally when we're looking at prototypes.
zh: 所以是的，我在想设计师如何能更像 VC 一样，在内部当我们查看原型时。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is super interesting.
zh: 这非常有趣。
